title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: Your company's senior management team has asked you to produce a report on the impact of the hybrid working policy introduced eighteen months ago. Your report should assess the effects on employee productivity, team collaboration and staff wellbeing, and recommend whether the current policy should be maintained, extended or revised.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on the Impact of the Hybrid Working Policy**

**Introduction**
This report evaluates the outcomes of the hybrid working policy implemented eighteen months ago, drawing on staff survey data, productivity metrics and team leader feedback. It assesses effects across three dimensions — productivity, collaboration and wellbeing — and concludes with a policy recommendation.

**Productivity**
Staff survey results indicate that 71% of employees report equal or higher individual productivity when working from home, particularly those in roles requiring sustained analytical or creative focus. However, project completion data shows a 12% increase in average task turnaround times for cross-functional projects, suggesting that remote working has introduced coordination inefficiencies for team-dependent work.

**Collaboration**
Team leaders across four out of five departments have identified a measurable decline in spontaneous knowledge-sharing. New employees, in particular, have reported difficulty integrating into team networks, with onboarding satisfaction scores falling by 18% compared with the pre-hybrid period.

**Staff Wellbeing**
On the positive side, reported stress levels have decreased by 23%, and staff retention has improved, with voluntary turnover falling from 14% to 9% over the same period. These are significant gains.

**Recommendation**
It is recommended that the hybrid policy be maintained but revised to require a minimum of three office days per week for staff in collaborative or client-facing roles, while retaining full flexibility for roles that are primarily independent. Structured in-person onboarding for new starters should also be introduced.

**Conclusion**
A targeted revision will preserve wellbeing benefits while addressing identified collaboration deficits.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: Your manager has asked for your views on a proposal to require all staff to return to the office five days a week, reversing the current hybrid working arrangement. Write an email to your manager expressing your concerns about the proposal, acknowledging the reasons given for it, and suggesting a compromise arrangement that could address those concerns while preserving some flexibility for employees.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Subject: Response to Full Office Return Proposal

Dear Ms Thornton,

Thank you for sharing the proposal regarding a full return to office working and for inviting staff feedback before a final decision is made. I appreciate the transparency, and I would like to offer my perspective constructively.

I fully understand the rationale behind the proposal. The concerns you have raised about declining in-person collaboration, the integration challenges faced by newer team members, and the need to reinforce a shared team identity are all legitimate and reflect issues that several of us have observed. I do not wish to dismiss them.

However, I have significant reservations about a full five-day return. Many colleagues — particularly those with long commutes or caring responsibilities — have restructured their lives around the current hybrid arrangement, and an abrupt reversal risks damaging the staff wellbeing and retention improvements we have worked hard to achieve. Two colleagues in our team have already indicated privately that they would reconsider their employment if fully remote working were eliminated entirely.

With this in mind, I would like to suggest a structured compromise: a requirement of three in-office days per week, with two designated as team anchor days on which all members are expected to be present. This would rebuild the face-to-face interaction that the proposal seeks to restore while preserving sufficient flexibility to protect the wellbeing gains of the current policy.

I would welcome the opportunity to discuss this further at your convenience.

Kind regards,
Marco Delacroix

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a book called 'The Home Office Advantage' which argues that remote working is unambiguously beneficial for both employees and organisations. Write a review for a business magazine, evaluating the strength of the author's arguments, assessing whether the book presents a balanced view, and recommending it to your readers.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Persuasive but Partial: A Review of 'The Home Office Advantage'**

'The Home Office Advantage' by Petra Yamamoto arrives at a moment of genuine uncertainty about the future of work, and it makes its central argument — that remote working is unambiguously good for both employees and organisations — with considerable energy and a wealth of supporting data.

Yamamoto's strengths are most evident in her treatment of individual productivity. Drawing on a broad range of longitudinal studies and real-world case studies from technology firms, she builds a compelling case that employees in focused, independent roles produce higher-quality work when freed from the interruptions of open-plan offices. Her chapter on the wellbeing benefits of eliminating the daily commute is particularly well-evidenced and genuinely persuasive.

However, the book's central weakness is its reluctance to engage seriously with the counterevidence. The considerable body of research on the collaborative and cultural costs of sustained remote working receives only a brief, dismissive treatment, and Yamamoto's case studies are drawn almost exclusively from the technology sector, a context that does not transfer straightforwardly to manufacturing, healthcare, retail or education. The book's subtitle promises to address 'all organisations', but its scope is in practice far narrower.

Readers looking for an enthusiastic, evidence-backed case for remote working will find much to value here. Those seeking a genuinely balanced assessment of the trade-offs involved in different working arrangements will need to look further. 'The Home Office Advantage' is recommended with that important caveat firmly in mind.
